journal-title-group.journal-title: Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery Global Open

title-group.article-title: Posture-based Assessment: Thread Lift Versus High-intensity Focused Ultrasound in Asians

contrib-group.aff: ‡Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, Juntendo University School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan
§Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, National Center for Global Health and Medicine, Tokyo, Japan
**Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital, London, England
††Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, St Mary’s Hospital, London, England
‡‡Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, Bradford Teaching Hospital, Bradford, England.

author-notes.corresp: Takako Nakao, MD, Lux Clinic, 6 floor ACN Shinjuku Building, 8-12 2 Chome Shinjuku, Shinjuku, Tokyo 160-0022, Japan, E-mail: takotakotaako330@gmail.com

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non Commercial-No Derivatives License 4.0 (CCBY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Minimally invasive facial laxity treatments, such as thread lifts and high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU), are increasingly popular in Asia, but standardized guidelines are lacking. Treatment outcomes are often subjective and heavily dependent on the physician’s expertise. Earlier studies indicate that facial soft tissue shifts significantly between supine and seated positions in cases of laxity. This study aimed to objectively evaluate facial laxity improvements after thread lift and HIFU treatments in Asians.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: Photographs of 143 Japanese individuals (mean age, 41.0 ± 15.5 y; mean body mass index, 21.2 ± 3.2 kg/m2) taken between February and May 2024 were analyzed. Frontal and lateral facial measurements in both seated and supine positions were compared across untreated, HIFU, and thread lift groups using ImageJ software. Statistical significance was determined using t tests with a threshold value of P less than 0.05.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: The soft tissue shifts were measured as 20.8% (SD = 19.4) in untreated, 20.7% (SD = 14.1) in HIFU, and 14.1% (SD = 11.6) in thread lift groups for frontal views. Lateral views showed shifts of 41.4% (SD = 20.9), 38.2% (SD = 20.7), and 33.4% (SD = 23.2), respectively. Significant reductions in tissue movement were observed in the thread lift group (frontal, P = 0.0092; lateral, P = 0.02) but not in the HIFU treatment group.

sec.title: Conclusions:
sec.p: Thread lifting significantly reduces facial tissue movement between seated and supine positions, indicating superior efficacy in preventing facial laxity. This study underscored the importance of assessing facial laxity in the seated position to accurately gauge treatment effects.

sec.title: Takeaways
sec.p: Question: How does soft tissue movement between sitting and supine positions affect outcomes of thread lift and high-intensity focused ultrasound treatments for facial laxity in Asian patients?

Findings: In 138 Japanese participants, thread lifts significantly reduced soft tissue movement, particularly in normal or underweight individuals. Obese patients showed less improvement, highlighting body mass index–dependent effects. Posture during evaluation and procedures influenced outcomes.

Meaning: Thread lifts effectively address facial laxity in Asian populations, with body mass index and posture playing key roles. This study underscored the importance of individualized treatment approaches and assessing outcomes in the sitting position to optimize aesthetic results.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: In recent years, cosmetic procedures have gained rapid popularity across Asia, particularly in Japan and South Korea. The expansion of social media platforms has greatly facilitated access to information on cosmetic treatments, allowing individuals to view realistic pre- and postprocedure photographs, which has driven a growing demand for aesthetic enhancements.1–4 Among the many available treatments, minimally invasive methods for facial skin laxity, such as thread lifts and high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU), have become increasingly popular due to their relatively low invasiveness compared with surgery.5–8 However, standardized guidelines for laxity treatments remain absent. Facial soft tissue consists of complex, multilayered structures with variable thickness, elasticity, and mobility; even small changes in these layers can significantly impact an individual’s appearance. Accurate assessment of treatment outcomes is therefore crucial. Notably, previous studies have reported that facial soft tissue positions vary between the supine and sitting positions.9–12 This raises concerns about the accuracy of supine-position assessments in predicting outcomes in the upright position.

This study aimed to evaluate changes in facial soft tissue movement between seated and supine positions in Asian patients undergoing thread lift and HIFU treatments for facial laxity, with the goal of informing optimal treatment approaches for improved aesthetic outcomes.

sec.title: MATERIALS AND METHODS
sec.p: This study analyzed photographs of 143 Japanese volunteers taken from February to May 2024. Patients with previous surgical procedures that could cause significant facial soft tissue scarring, such as facelifts or rhinoplasties, were excluded. None of the patients had a history of cancer or connective tissue disease. Of the initial participants, 138 were included after ensuring image clarity and consistent posture in pre- and posttreatment photographs (Table 1). Photographs were taken in frontal and lateral views in both seated and supine positions.

During imaging, patients positioned their heads so that the forehead’s midpoint aligned vertically with the camera, and their head and chin were horizontally aligned, maintaining a relaxed expression with lips lightly closed. For supine images, heads were kept horizontal without using pillows. Cases where slight head tilt or indistinct lower jaw contours hindered measurement accuracy were excluded. The distance from the camera to the patient was maintained at 2 feet (standard clinical photography distance), with a Canon PowerShot G7 X Mark II (Canon Inc., Minato, Tokyo, Japan) set to a 50-mm focal length, approximating human visual acuity.13–15 The camera was mounted on a fixed stand with the lens height adjusted to eye level, and the nose-to-lens distance was verified with a ruler (Fig. 1).

caption.p: Front and side images were captured in sitting and supine positions using a fixed camera stand, with the lens height aligned to the subject’s eye level. Frontal view of sitting (A), lateral view of sitting (B), and supine position (C).

sec.p: Measurements included the vertical distance from the central corneal reflex to the mandibular border in the frontal view, normalized by the palpebral fissure width and expressed as a percentage. In the lateral view, the vertical distance from the lateral palpebral fissure edge to the mandibular border was divided by the length from the porion to the lateral canthus. All images were analyzed using ImageJ software to calculate average and SD values (Figs. 2, 3).

caption.p: The point and length to measure. A, In the frontal view, the blue and green lines (eye center to facial contour) were compared with the yellow eyelid crease width as a reference (blue or green/yellow), ×100. B, In the lateral view, the purple line (lateral eyelid cleft to chin) was compared with the red cleft-to-auricle distance as a reference (purple/red), ×100.
An 84-year-old female volunteer. A, Frontal photograph in a sitting position. B, Frontal photograph in a supine position.

sec.p: We defined greater measurement differences between sitting and supine positions as indicating a higher degree of soft tissue movement, correlating with increased facial laxity. Statistical significance between positional differences was determined using a t test in Microsoft Excel for Mac version 16.87 (Microsoft Corp., Redmond, WA), with a P value of less than 0.05 considered statistically significant.

In this study, participants were divided into 2 groups: those with prior cosmetic treatment experience and those without, and their experiences with HIFU and thread lift procedures were compared. For the experienced group, only photographs of volunteers who had undergone HIFU or thread lift at least 3 months before the study were analyzed. In the case of HIFU, we selected participants who had received approximately 400 shots across the entire face. The number of inserted threads varied among individuals, but all participants in the thread lift group had at least 3 threads inserted per side. Additionally, the procedures were performed by different surgeons. The technique for inserting threads involves placing them in the subcutaneous fat layer and above the superficial fascia, ensuring that the threads do not catch on the dermis and do not cause skin irregularities. (See figure, Supplemental Digital Content 1, which displays a graphic showing how in recent years, most thread lifts in Japan use cogged threads that when pulled cranially after insertion, lift and secure the facial soft tissue. As the threads dissolve, linear scar formation helps to maintain the lift and prolong the improvement in sagging, https://links.lww.com/PRSGO/E403.) This is a common consensus when using threads, and the number and location of threads are determined by the surgeon based on the patient’s degree of laxity.16 Patients who underwent HIFU treatment received treatment on the midface, lower face, glabella, and under the chin. The neck was not treated. The number of HIFU sessions varied among individuals, but only those who had undergone treatment at least twice, with a frequency of once per year, were included in the study.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: Of the 143 volunteers, a total of 138 Japanese participants were included in the final analysis. Ages ranged from 21 to 88 years (mean, 41.0 y), with 108 women and 35 men. Body mass index (BMI) ranged from 15.4 to 29.9 kg/m2 (mean, 21.2 kg/m2). Regarding previous cosmetic procedures for reducing facial laxity, 84 participants had not received any treatment; 24 had undergone HIFU alone; and 52 had previously received thread lift treatments, regardless of thread type, period of treatment, number of treatments, or threads inserted (Table 1). All procedures were performed by different physicians and adhered to ethical standards in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.

When comparing absolute differences in measurements between the supine and sitting positions in both frontal and lateral images, the untreated group showed a mean soft tissue shift of 20.8% (SD = 19.4) for frontal images and 41.4% (SD = 20.9) for lateral images. In the HIFU group, the mean shifts were 20.7% (SD = 14.1) in the frontal images and 38.2% (SD = 20.7) in the lateral images. The thread lift group exhibited a shift of 14.1% (SD = 11.6) in frontal images and 33.4% (SD = 23.2) in lateral images. No significant difference was observed in the HIFU group compared with that in the untreated group (frontal, P = 0.48; lateral, P = 0.17). In contrast, the thread lift group demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in soft tissue movement compared with the untreated group (frontal, P = 0.0092; lateral, P = 0.02) (Figs. 4, 5).

caption.p: Differences in sitting and supine positions between those who had HIFU and those who did not.
Differences in sitting and supine positions between those who had thread lift and those who did not.

sec.p: Further analysis categorized participants by BMI as underweight (<18.5 kg/m2), normal weight (18.5–24.9 kg/m2), and overweight (≥25 kg/m2) to explore differences within these groups (Table 2). In the underweight category, 16 participants were untreated and 5 had previously undergone thread lift. In this category, untreated participants showed a mean shift of 16.5% (SD = 13.9) in frontal images and 41.1% (SD = 22.1) in lateral images compared with 7.97% (SD = 6.79) and 26.1% (SD = 11.8) in the thread lift group, respectively. These results indicated a significant difference in favor of the thread lift group (frontal, P = 0.0347; lateral, P = 0.0236) (Fig. 6).

caption.p: Comparison of thread lift in treated and untreated groups with BMI ≤18.5 kg/m2.

sec.p: Among those with normal weight, 58 participants were untreated and 18 had undergone thread lift. In frontal images, the untreated group had a mean shift of 22.5% (SD = 21.4) compared with 16.1% (SD = 12.5) in the thread lift group, whereas in lateral images, the untreated group had a mean shift of 41.2% (SD = 19.6) compared with 32.4% (SD = 25.0) in the thread lift group. Again, the thread lift group demonstrated significantly reduced soft tissue movement compared with the untreated group (frontal, P = 0.0449; lateral, P = 0.0156) (Fig. 7). In the overweight category, 10 participants were untreated and 3 had undergone thread lift. For frontal images, the untreated group had a mean shift of 17.9% (SD = 13.6) compared with 12.1% (SD = 9.24) in the thread lift group, whereas in lateral images, the mean shift was 43.7% (SD = 26.2) in the untreated group and 51.4% (SD = 18.7) in the thread lift group. No statistically significant difference was observed between the untreated and thread lift groups in this BMI category (frontal, P = 0.1675; lateral, P = 0.2542) (Fig. 8). These findings indicate that in overweight individuals (BMI ≥25 kg/m2), there was substantial soft tissue movement between the sitting and supine positions; even after thread lift treatment for facial laxity, the skin remained relatively loose in this group.

caption.p: Comparison of thread lift in treated and untreated groups with 18.5 < BMI < 25 kg/m2.
Comparison of thread lift in treated and untreated groups with BMI ≥25 kg/m2.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: This study suggests that thread lifting is more effective than HIFU alone in reducing the mobility of facial laxity. When examining the differences in soft tissue movement between sitting and supine positions, approximately 20%–40% movement was observed. Additionally, analysis by BMI revealed that thread lifting was less effective in preventing laxity in obese patients compared with those with normal or underweight BMIs.

Accurate pre- and postsurgical evaluation of facial soft tissue is essential in aesthetic medicine, regardless of which procedure is being performed.17 Recently, many clinics have started to use photography and 3-dimensional (3D) imaging to compare surgical outcomes.9 In addition, many cosmetic procedures are performed in the supine position, and the effects of gravity on the soft tissues are different from those on the face in the upright position, which is the position we spend most of our daily lives in. Previous studies have reported that the position of the soft tissues of the face differs between the supine and sitting positions.9–12

In general, Asians have a different skeletal structure than Westerners, and they also have more soft tissue in their cheeks. However, Asian skin tends to be thicker and more elastic due to dermal structure, and it is known that the effects of sagging generally appear later in Asians.18 However, research on soft tissue displacement under gravitational influence in Asian populations remains limited.11 Given that facial soft tissue positioning shifts based on patient posture, it is essential to account for these effects during facial procedures.9 Our findings confirmed significant differences in soft tissue positioning between sitting and supine postures, highlighting the importance of patient posture during both evaluation and surgery. This is particularly relevant in cosmetic surgery where even minor changes in soft tissue can affect outcomes, and surgeons must therefore consider posture and tissue state throughout all stages of the procedure. For example, face-lifting procedures typically performed in the supine position under anesthesia may overlook the gravitational displacement that would be apparent in an upright position. Consequently, evaluating soft tissue in the sitting position during surgery is recommended.

Because patients spend more time upright than supine, procedures performed with gravitational influence in mind may result in higher satisfaction. Treatments involving facial reshaping or simple injections, such as hyaluronic acid, would ideally be performed in or checked in the sitting position to achieve optimal results, as suggested by this study.

Because patients spend most of their daily lives in an upright position, preprocedural marking in this position is recommended to achieve the intended postoperative outcome. Marking in the supine position may lead to discrepancies when the patient returns to an upright posture, as the soft tissue shifts.

HIFU, which stimulates skin tightening by targeting subcutaneous tissue and superficial musculo-aponeurotic system fascia layers with focused ultrasound, has become popular due to its minimal downtime and skin-tightening effects.5,19 Numerous other skin-tightening and resurfacing options, such as monopolar, bipolar, and tripolar radiofrequency, have emerged, providing a variety of noninvasive treatments for aging skin.20,21 Recently, thermal approaches such as radiofrequency and laser procedures, as well as topical applications, have gained popularity in Asia.22 HIFU, similar to these thermal approaches, has shown beneficial effects in skin tightening and remains a common procedure in cosmetic clinics.

However, our findings indicated no significant difference in outcomes for patients who had undergone HIFU alone compared with those who had not received any treatment. In contrast, patients who received thread lift treatment showed significant differences in soft tissue movement compared with untreated patients (Figs. 4, 5). This suggests that thread lifting may be a more effective option for addressing laxity in Asian faces, even for patients desiring minimally invasive alternatives to facelifts. Furthermore, the comparison by BMI showed that thread lifting was more effective in individuals with normal or underweight BMIs (Figs. 6, 7). In individuals with low subcutaneous fat, threads may be inserted at a shallower depth, making them more noticeable in the early postoperative period.16,23 However, in this study, all patients underwent thread lift procedures using absorbable threads. Because these threads are designed to integrate with the surrounding tissue over time, no patients in our study, including those with a low BMI, exhibited visible thread insertion sites on the skin surface beyond 3 months postoperatively. In contrast, thread lift effectiveness was reduced in obese patients (Fig. 8). Previous studies using 3D imaging reported that soft tissue displacement varies with posture and body shape,12 which aligns with our findings that thread lifting outcomes depend on soft tissue volume. Based on these results, recommending additional threads for obese patients or advising weight reduction before thread lifting may improve outcomes for these individuals. HIFU targets deep tissue layers to stimulate collagen production and contraction, but in individuals with thicker subcutaneous fat, ultrasound energy may not effectively reach the target layers. Similarly, thread lifts reposition soft tissue and induce fibrosis for support, but excess fat may exert gravitational force, reducing the lifting effect.

These findings suggest that stronger suspension techniques or combination therapies may be needed for optimal results in high-BMI patients. Although BMI is widely used to indicate body composition, it does not always reflect facial soft tissue volume, as factors such as muscle mass, fat distribution, and metabolic conditions influence BMI. Additionally, aging and nutrition impact connective tissue integrity and elasticity, independent of BMI. Despite its limitations, BMI remains a practical tool for categorizing patients and analyzing soft tissue mobility trends. Future studies should incorporate precise facial fat measurements, such as ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging, to better assess the relationship between body composition and soft tissue movement.

This study has several limitations. The sample size was relatively small, and only 2-dimensional images were used for evaluation, although future studies using 3D imaging may provide more detailed assessments of soft tissue.24 Also, slight head position differences due to individual balance during photography may affect accuracy.24

This retrospective study lacked a predetermined design and sample size calculation. Group size differences, particularly the small high-BMI sample, may have influenced results. The statistical analysis estimates trends rather than providing definitive conclusions. Future prospective studies with larger, balanced samples are needed for confirmation.

A major limitation is the lack of detailed data on previous thread lift treatments, including the number, location, and timing. In this study, the number and type of threads used for thread lifting varied depending on the surgeon and were not standardized. Although we evaluated the general preventive effect of thread lifting on facial laxity, future prospective studies with standardized thread types and quantities are warranted to further validate and refine these findings. As absorbable threads induce fibrosis, previous treatments may affect soft tissue stabilization. Future research should incorporate these factors to better assess their impact on facial laxity prevention.

With clinical imaging technology advancing rapidly, 3D imaging is broadening the scope of assessment methods in aesthetic, plastic, and maxillofacial surgery.11 Future studies are anticipated to use these technologies to more accurately evaluate soft tissue displacement and facial changes after cosmetic procedures.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: This study confirms that thread lifting is more effective than HIFU alone for preventing facial laxity, especially in patients with normal or underweight BMIs. Approximately 20%–40% of soft tissue movement occurs between sitting and supine positions, highlighting the critical role of posture in aesthetic evaluations and procedures. Although thread lifting enhances facial contour and satisfaction, its effectiveness is reduced in obese patients, requiring tailored strategies. The study emphasizes the need for patient-specific approaches and suggests future research incorporating 3D imaging to better assess soft tissue movement and outcomes across diverse populations.

sec.title: DISCLOSURE
sec.p: The authors have no financial interest to declare in relation to the content of this article.

fn.p: Presented at the Plastic Aesthetic Surgery Meeting, November 9 and 10, 2024, Singapore; and Aesthetic Plastic Surgery Korea, April 19 and 20, 2025, Seoul, Korea.

p.italic: Disclosure statements are at the end of this article, following the correspondence information.

fn.p: Related Digital Media are available in the full-text version of the article on www.PRSGlobalOpen.com.
